title: The Plumber Problem: Why Huang Says the Real AI Bottleneck Has Nothing to Do With Chips

source.original: Jensen Huang – Will Nvidia's moat persist?

body_html:
When pressed on whether upstream semiconductor supply can keep pace with Nvidia's doubling revenue trajectory, Jensen Huang offered a surprising answer: the hardest constraint isn't fabs, packaging, or even EUV machines. It's plumbers and electricians. The physical infrastructure required to build and cool data centers at scale depends on tradespeople whose supply cannot be doubled with a purchase order or a demand signal to a CEO.

Huang draws a sharp distinction between bottlenecks that respond to industrial scaling signals and those that don't. CoWoS packaging, once a critical chokepoint, was resolved within two years because the industry "swarmed the living daylights out of it." TSMC now scales packaging capacity in lockstep with logic demand. HBM memory followed a similar arc after Huang personally convinced Micron's leadership to double down years before the market validated the bet. These are problems that yield to capital, conviction, and coordination.

But the downstream infrastructure—the actual buildings, cooling systems, and electrical work—depends on labor markets that don't respond to demand signals the same way. Huang half-jokingly noted that plumbers are invited to next year's GTC. The deeper point is that energy policy and physical construction timelines are fundamentally different from semiconductor manufacturing cycles. You can plan a new CoWoS line in months; permitting and building a gigawatt data center takes years.

This framing reorders the conventional hierarchy of AI scaling constraints. Most analysts focus on chip supply, HBM availability, or model efficiency. Huang argues these are all two-to-three year problems solvable with demand signals and investment. The true long-term bottleneck is the physical and regulatory infrastructure beneath the entire stack—energy availability, construction labor, and the policy environment that governs both.

The implication for investors and policymakers is stark: the AI industry's growth rate may ultimately be governed not by Moore's Law or algorithmic breakthroughs, but by how fast democratic societies can permit power plants and train electricians. Huang frames this as the one constraint Nvidia's engineering prowess cannot directly solve.
